---
title: "CEClust"
output: github_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  eval = FALSE
)
```

# CEClust

`CEClust` implements composite entropy clustering for Gaussian, categorical,
and mixed data. The central practical object is the empirical CEC phase diagram:
one explores how the fitted partition changes over a grid of density bounds
`C` and entropy weights `lambda`.

The two main tuning parameters are:

- `lambda`, which controls the entropy regularisation and therefore the
  granularity of the partition;
- `C`, a density bound for the reference functions. Cells where fitted
  components reach this bound are flagged by the saturation diagnostic.

The package is intentionally used through a small public API:

```r
CECclassif(...)          # one C and one lambda
CECfitLambdaGrid(...)    # one C and several lambdas
CECfitBoundGrid(...)     # several C values and several lambdas

CECplotGrid(...)         # C x lambda phase diagram
CECplotPath(...)         # lambda path for one C
CECplotPartition(...)    # selected partition
CECexplore(...)          # Shiny explorer

CECsummariseGrid(...)
CECselectStableLambdas(...)
CECextractPartition(...)
```

By default, grid objects are compact: the package keeps selected partitions,
fitted parameters, and diagnostics, while avoiding large internal copies of
assignment matrices.

All examples below are written as explicit steps. They do not call a generic
"simulate + fit + plot" wrapper: the data generation, the lambda-grid fit, the
`C x lambda` grid fit, the summaries, the extraction, and the plots are shown
separately. In this README the code chunks are not evaluated automatically, so
the interactive explorer and the larger grids are never launched during package
checks.

## Installation

From GitHub:

```r
remotes::install_github("dumont-thierry/CEClust")
```

From a local source folder:

```r
install.packages(".", repos = NULL, type = "source")
```

Optional packages extend the workflow:

- `shiny` for `CECexplore()`;
- `ggplot2` for some multivariate displays;
- `mclust` for optional ICL comparisons;
- `knitr` and `rmarkdown` for vignettes.

## Common Reading Pattern

For each data set, the same pattern is useful.

First, inspect a single-`C` lambda path:

```r
lambda_path <- CECfitLambdaGrid(
  Z = Z,
  C = C_reference,
  lambda_grid = lambda_grid,
  r0 = 10,
  k0 = 10,
  B = 10,
  Nshots_fresh = 10,
  Nshots_warm = 10,
  Nloop = 100,
  familyType = family_type,
  seed = 13,
  n_cores = 2,
  checkpoint_dir = FALSE,
  auto_checkpoint = FALSE,
  show_progress = FALSE
)

head(lambda_path$summary)
```

Then scan a full `C x lambda` grid:

```r
grid <- CECfitBoundGrid(
  Z = Z,
  lambda_grid = lambda_grid,
  C_grid = C_grid,
  r0 = 10,
  k0 = 10,
  B = 10,
  Nshots_fresh = 10,
  Nshots_warm = 10,
  Nloop = 100,
  familyType = family_type,
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.95,
  sat_threshold = 0,
  n_cores = 2,
  compact_results = TRUE
)

CECplotGrid(grid)
CECexplore(grid)
```

The examples below instantiate this pattern with grids adapted to each setting.

## Uniform Distribution, n = 100

This example is the sanity check: the sample is uniform on `[0, 1]`, so one
does not expect a meaningful intrinsic clustering structure. The phase diagram
is mainly used to see where fragmentation is driven by the interaction between
small `lambda` and the density bound `C`.

```r
library(CEClust)

set.seed(13)
Z_uniform <- runif(100)

lambda_uniform <- seq(0.1, 2, by = 0.1)
C_uniform <- seq(0.5, 10, by = 0.5)
```

Start with the lambda path at a fixed, interpretable value of `C`.

```r
uniform_lambda_path <- CECfitLambdaGrid(
  Z = Z_uniform,
  C = 10,
  lambda_grid = lambda_uniform,
  r0 = 10,
  k0 = 10,
  B = 10,
  Nshots_fresh = 10,
  Nshots_warm = 10,
  Nloop = 100,
  familyType = "gaussUniv",
  seed = 13,
  n_cores = 2,
  checkpoint_dir = FALSE,
  auto_checkpoint = FALSE,
  show_progress = FALSE
)

# Diagnostic table along the lambda path:
head(uniform_lambda_path$summary)

# Extract one representative partition per lambda.
uniform_best <- CECextractBestPartitions(
  uniform_lambda_path,
  source = "fixed",
  criterion = "projected_H",
  Z = Z_uniform
)

head(uniform_best$summary)
```

Now scan the full `C x lambda` grid.

```r
uniform_grid <- CECfitBoundGrid(
  Z = Z_uniform,
  dataset_name = "uniform_0_1_n100",
  lambda_grid = lambda_uniform,
  C_grid = C_uniform,
  r0 = 10,
  k0 = 10,
  B = 10,
  Nshots_fresh = 10,
  Nshots_warm = 10,
  Nloop = 100,
  familyType = "gaussUniv",
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.95,
  sat_threshold = 0,
  n_cores = 2,
  compact_results = TRUE
)

uniform_summary <- CECsummariseGrid(uniform_grid)
head(uniform_summary)

CECplotGrid(
  uniform_grid,
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.95,
  sat_threshold = 0
)

CECplotPath(
  uniform_grid,
  C = 10,
  type = "partitions",
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.95,
  sat_threshold = 0
)

uniform_stable <- CECselectStableLambdas(
  uniform_grid,
  C = 10,
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.95,
  sat_threshold = 0
)

uniform_stable$kept_lambdas
uniform_stable$summary

# Extract the partition at one grid cell chosen after reading the diagram.
uniform_partition <- CECextractPartition(
  uniform_grid,
  C = 10,
  lambda = 1
)

table(uniform_partition)

CECexplore(uniform_grid)
```

## One-Dimensional Seven-Class Gaussian Mixture, n = 100

This example is a one-dimensional mixture with seven generating classes. The
generating labels are kept only for display and interpretation; the CEC fit uses
only the numeric coordinate.

```r
library(CEClust)

simulate_mixture_1d_7 <- function(n, seed = 13) {
  set.seed(seed)

  weights <- c(0.12, 0.10, 0.18, 0.16, 0.14, 0.17, 0.13)
  means <- c(-5.0, -3.2, -1.6, 0.0, 1.4, 3.0, 4.8)
  sds <- c(0.28, 0.22, 0.35, 0.45, 0.30, 0.32, 0.26)

  z_true <- sample.int(length(weights), size = n, replace = TRUE, prob = weights)
  z <- rnorm(n, mean = means[z_true], sd = sds[z_true])

  data.frame(
    z = z,
    true_class = factor(z_true, levels = seq_along(weights))
  )
}

mixture_1d_7 <- simulate_mixture_1d_7(n = 100, seed = 13)
Z_1d_7 <- mixture_1d_7$z

lambda_1d_7 <- seq(0.05, 1.5, length.out = 20)
C_1d_7 <- seq(0.5, 10, by = 0.5)
```

Fit the lambda path first. This is useful when one wants to understand the
resolution effect at a fixed density bound.

```r
mixture_1d_7_lambda_path <- CECfitLambdaGrid(
  Z = Z_1d_7,
  C = 6,
  lambda_grid = lambda_1d_7,
  r0 = 10,
  k0 = 10,
  B = 10,
  Nshots_fresh = 10,
  Nshots_warm = 10,
  Nloop = 100,
  familyType = "gaussUniv",
  seed = 13,
  n_cores = 2,
  checkpoint_dir = FALSE,
  auto_checkpoint = FALSE,
  show_progress = FALSE
)

head(mixture_1d_7_lambda_path$summary)

mixture_1d_7_best <- CECextractBestPartitions(
  mixture_1d_7_lambda_path,
  source = "fixed",
  criterion = "projected_H",
  Z = Z_1d_7
)

head(mixture_1d_7_best$summary)
```

Then fit the full phase diagram.

```r
mixture_1d_7_grid <- CECfitBoundGrid(
  Z = Z_1d_7,
  display_data = mixture_1d_7,
  labels = mixture_1d_7$true_class,
  label_name = "Generating class",
  dataset_name = "gaussian_mixture_1d_7_n100",
  lambda_grid = lambda_1d_7,
  C_grid = C_1d_7,
  r0 = 10,
  k0 = 10,
  B = 10,
  Nshots_fresh = 10,
  Nshots_warm = 10,
  Nloop = 100,
  familyType = "gaussUniv",
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.95,
  sat_threshold = 0,
  n_cores = 2,
  compact_results = TRUE
)

mixture_1d_7_summary <- CECsummariseGrid(mixture_1d_7_grid)
head(mixture_1d_7_summary)

CECplotGrid(
  mixture_1d_7_grid,
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.95,
  sat_threshold = 0
)

CECplotPath(
  mixture_1d_7_grid,
  C = 6,
  type = "partitions",
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.95,
  sat_threshold = 0
)

mixture_1d_7_stable <- CECselectStableLambdas(
  mixture_1d_7_grid,
  C = 6,
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.95,
  sat_threshold = 0
)

mixture_1d_7_stable$kept_lambdas
mixture_1d_7_stable$summary

# Choose a cell after inspecting the phase diagram.
mixture_1d_7_partition <- CECextractPartition(
  mixture_1d_7_grid,
  C = 6,
  lambda = 0.5
)

table(
  CEC_cluster = mixture_1d_7_partition,
  generating_class = mixture_1d_7$true_class
)

CECplotPartition(
  mixture_1d_7_grid,
  C = 6,
  lambda = 0.5
)

CECexplore(mixture_1d_7_grid)
```

## Two-Dimensional Gaussian Mixture, n = 100

This example uses a two-dimensional mixture with one compact group, one broad
central group, and one elongated group. Again, labels are supplied only for
visualisation and evaluation.

```r
library(CEClust)

simulate_mixture_2d <- function(n, seed = 13) {
  set.seed(seed)

  weights <- c(0.45, 0.25, 0.30)
  means <- list(c(-2, 2), c(0, 0), c(5, -6))
  covariances <- list(
    0.16 * diag(2),
    5.76 * diag(2),
    matrix(c(8.5, 7.5, 7.5, 8.5), nrow = 2)
  )

  z_true <- sample.int(3, size = n, replace = TRUE, prob = weights)
  Z <- matrix(NA_real_, nrow = n, ncol = 2)

  for (j in seq_along(weights)) {
    idx <- which(z_true == j)
    if (!length(idx)) next

    eig <- eigen(covariances[[j]], symmetric = TRUE)
    transform <- eig$vectors %*% diag(sqrt(pmax(eig$values, 0)), nrow = 2)
    noise <- matrix(rnorm(2 * length(idx)), ncol = 2)
    Z[idx, ] <- matrix(means[[j]], nrow = length(idx), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE) +
      noise %*% t(transform)
  }

  colnames(Z) <- c("z1", "z2")
  data.frame(
    z1 = Z[, 1],
    z2 = Z[, 2],
    true_component = factor(z_true, labels = c("compact", "central", "elongated"))
  )
}

mixture_2d <- simulate_mixture_2d(n = 100, seed = 13)
Z_2d <- mixture_2d[, c("z1", "z2")]

lambda_2d <- seq(3 / 20, 3, length.out = 20)
C_2d <- seq(1 / 10, 2, length.out = 20)
```

A lambda path at a fixed `C` is useful for inspecting how the 2D partition
changes when only the entropy weight varies.

```r
mixture_2d_lambda_path <- CECfitLambdaGrid(
  Z = Z_2d,
  C = 1.5,
  lambda_grid = lambda_2d,
  r0 = 10,
  k0 = 10,
  B = 10,
  Nshots_fresh = 10,
  Nshots_warm = 10,
  Nloop = 100,
  familyType = "gaussVector",
  seed = 13,
  n_cores = 2,
  checkpoint_dir = FALSE,
  auto_checkpoint = FALSE,
  show_progress = FALSE
)

head(mixture_2d_lambda_path$summary)

mixture_2d_best <- CECextractBestPartitions(
  mixture_2d_lambda_path,
  source = "fixed",
  criterion = "projected_H",
  Z = Z_2d
)

head(mixture_2d_best$summary)
```

Now fit the full `C x lambda` grid.

```r
mixture_2d_grid <- CECfitBoundGrid(
  Z = Z_2d,
  display_data = mixture_2d,
  labels = mixture_2d$true_component,
  label_name = "Generating component",
  dataset_name = "gaussian_mixture_2d_n100",
  lambda_grid = lambda_2d,
  C_grid = C_2d,
  r0 = 10,
  k0 = 10,
  B = 10,
  Nshots_fresh = 10,
  Nshots_warm = 10,
  Nloop = 100,
  familyType = "gaussVector",
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.95,
  sat_threshold = 0,
  n_cores = 2,
  compact_results = TRUE
)

mixture_2d_summary <- CECsummariseGrid(mixture_2d_grid)
head(mixture_2d_summary)

CECplotGrid(
  mixture_2d_grid,
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.95,
  sat_threshold = 0
)

CECplotPath(
  mixture_2d_grid,
  C = 1.5,
  type = "summary",
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.95,
  sat_threshold = 0
)

mixture_2d_stable <- CECselectStableLambdas(
  mixture_2d_grid,
  C = 1.5,
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.95,
  sat_threshold = 0
)

mixture_2d_stable$kept_lambdas
mixture_2d_stable$summary

# Choose a representative cell after reading the phase diagram.
mixture_2d_partition <- CECextractPartition(
  mixture_2d_grid,
  C = 1.5,
  lambda = 1.0
)

table(
  CEC_cluster = mixture_2d_partition,
  generating_component = mixture_2d$true_component
)

CECplotPartition(
  mixture_2d_grid,
  C = 1.5,
  lambda = 1.0,
  x_var = "z1",
  y_var = "z2",
  shape_var = "true_component"
)

CECexplore(mixture_2d_grid)
```

## Iris With Labels for Visualisation and Evaluation

In this example, the clustering variables are the four quantitative iris
measurements. `Species` is passed only as metadata, so it can be used in plots
and contingency tables; it is not a clustering variable.

```r
library(CEClust)

Z_iris <- iris[, 1:4]
display_iris <- iris

lambda_iris <- seq(0.4, 2.4, by = 0.2)
C_iris <- seq(1, 5, by = 0.5)
```

The lambda path at fixed `C` gives a first view of the resolution path.

```r
iris_lambda_path <- CECfitLambdaGrid(
  Z = Z_iris,
  C = 3,
  lambda_grid = lambda_iris,
  r0 = 10,
  k0 = 10,
  B = 10,
  Nshots_fresh = 10,
  Nshots_warm = 10,
  Nloop = 100,
  familyType = "gaussVector",
  seed = 13,
  n_cores = 2,
  checkpoint_dir = FALSE,
  auto_checkpoint = FALSE,
  show_progress = FALSE
)

head(iris_lambda_path$summary)

iris_best <- CECextractBestPartitions(
  iris_lambda_path,
  source = "fixed",
  criterion = "projected_H",
  Z = Z_iris
)

head(iris_best$summary)
```

Then fit the complete phase diagram.

```r
iris_grid <- CECfitBoundGrid(
  Z = Z_iris,
  display_data = display_iris,
  labels = iris$Species,
  label_name = "Species",
  dataset_name = "iris_with_labels_for_evaluation",
  lambda_grid = lambda_iris,
  C_grid = C_iris,
  r0 = 10,
  k0 = 10,
  B = 10,
  Nshots_fresh = 10,
  Nshots_warm = 10,
  Nloop = 100,
  familyType = "gaussVector",
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.9,
  sat_threshold = 0.05,
  n_cores = 2,
  compact_results = TRUE
)

iris_summary <- CECsummariseGrid(iris_grid)
head(iris_summary)

CECplotGrid(
  iris_grid,
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.9,
  sat_threshold = 0.05
)

CECplotPath(
  iris_grid,
  C = 3,
  type = "summary",
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.9,
  sat_threshold = 0.05
)

iris_stable <- CECselectStableLambdas(
  iris_grid,
  C = 3,
  stab_algo_threshold = 0.8,
  rsi_threshold = 0.9,
  sat_threshold = 0.05
)

iris_stable$kept_lambdas
iris_stable$summary

# Choose a representative cell after reading the grid and lambda path.
iris_partition <- CECextractPartition(
  iris_grid,
  C = 3,
  lambda = 1.0
)

# Species is used here only to evaluate or interpret the unsupervised partition.
table(
  CEC_cluster = iris_partition,
  Species = iris$Species
)

CECplotPartition(
  iris_grid,
  C = 3,
  lambda = 1.0,
  x_var = "Petal.Length",
  y_var = "Petal.Width",
  shape_var = "Species"
)

CECexplore(iris_grid)
```

## Practical Notes

The example grids above are deliberately moderate. For publication-quality phase
diagrams, increase `k0`, `B`, `Nshots_fresh`, and `Nshots_warm`, and use more
worker processes if available. For long runs, prefer explicit checkpoint
directories and run one data set at a time.

Useful diagnostic columns in `CECsummariseGrid()` include:

```r
summary_grid <- CECsummariseGrid(mixture_2d_grid)

summary_grid[
  order(summary_grid$C, summary_grid$lambda),
  c("C", "lambda", "REO", "stab_algo", "RSI", "sat")
]
```

Typical filtering rules are:

```r
admissible_cells <- subset(
  summary_grid,
  stab_algo > 0.8 & RSI > 0.95 & sat == 0
)

admissible_cells
```

The Shiny explorer remains the recommended interface for comparing neighbouring
cells and inspecting the associated partitions:

```r
CECexplore(mixture_2d_grid)
```
